items.instruction: Does this RVR requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers must furnish RVR values for the runway in use in accordance with paragraph 2-8-3 when RVR equipment is operational, irrespective of subsequent operation or nonoperation of navigational or visual aids for the application of RVR as takeoff or landing minima.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in the body of 2-8-1.

items.instruction: Does this numeric RVR statement match the note?
items.question_text: New equipment increments below 800 feet are 100 feet.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note in 2-8-1 states that new equipment increments below 800 feet are 100 feet.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Runway visual range equipment is operational for the runway in use, but a related visual aid later becomes unavailable. Another controller says the RVR no longer needs to be issued because the aid is out.
items.para_context: Under 2-8-1, if RVR equipment is operational, furnish the values for the runway in use in accordance with paragraph 2-8-3 regardless of subsequent operation or nonoperation of navigational or visual aids for use of RVR as takeoff or landing minima.

choices.text: Continue furnishing the RVR values for the runway in use.
Stop furnishing RVR because any later aid outage cancels the requirement.
Provide only visibility, not RVR, because the runway aid status changed.
Issue RVR only if the pilot requests it after taxi begins.

items.explanation: Per 2-8-1, operational RVR equipment drives the requirement to furnish RVR values even if related aids later become unavailable.

items.instruction: Is this volcanic-ash ground-ops directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: When volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, and to the extent possible, avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-1-14a.

items.instruction: Which action is specifically listed in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 3-1-14, which action is one of the listed procedures when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface?

choices.text: Provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures.
Require every taxiing aircraft to stop for spacing checks.
Suspend all departures until ash is no longer visible.
Route every aircraft back to the ramp for engine inspections.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-14b, provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Volcanic ash is present on the airport surface. A departure is ready, and another controller suggests stopping it at the hold line for an extended pause before takeoff to make spacing easier.
items.para_context: Under 3-1-14, when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface, avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing and provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures to the extent possible.

choices.text: Provide a rolling takeoff and avoid requiring an unnecessary full stop if possible.
Require a full stop because ash conditions make rolling movement less important.
Hold the aircraft until the pilot specifically requests a rolling takeoff.
Taxi the aircraft back to the gate because no departure movement is permitted.

items.explanation: Per 3-1-14, rolling movement is preferred to reduce ash becoming airborne again.

items.instruction: Does this ASDE requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: ASDE systems with safety logic must be operated continuously.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 3-6-1b1.

items.instruction: Which condition is one of the listed triggers for operating ASDE systems without safety logic?
items.question_text: Under 3-6-1, which condition is specifically listed for operating ASDE systems without safety logic?

choices.text: When visibility is less than the most distant point in the active movement area.
Only when RVR drops below takeoff minimums.
Only when a pilot requests surface radar assistance.
Only when an aircraft is on short final.

items.explanation: Per 3-6-1b2(b), ASDE systems without safety logic must be operated when visibility is less than the most distant point in the active movement area.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: The position relief briefing is complete, and you are assuming control of a position with ASDE. A trainee wants to postpone checking system status until traffic slows down later in the session.
items.para_context: Under 3-6-1a, the operational status of ASDE systems must be determined during the relief briefing or as soon as possible after assuming responsibility for the associated position.

choices.text: Determine the ASDE operational status now during the briefing or as soon as possible after assuming the position.
Delay checking the system until traffic slows because the briefing requirement is optional.
Use ASDE only after a surface alert occurs.
Ignore system status if visual observation appears adequate.

items.explanation: Per 3-6-1a, ASDE operational status is determined during the relief briefing or as soon as possible after assuming responsibility.

items.instruction: Which item is one required component of a degree-distance fix?
items.question_text: Under 4-4-3, which element is one of the required components of a degree-distance fix?

choices.text: Azimuth in degrees magnetic.
Estimated time over the fix.
Assigned transponder code.
Pilot-reported groundspeed.

items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a1, a degree-distance fix includes a location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, and distance in miles from the NAVAID used.

items.instruction: Does this degree-distance limit match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Below FL 180, the distance between fixes used to define the route must not exceed 80 miles.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a3(a), the limit below FL 180 is 80 miles.

items.instruction: Does this military routing requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Military aircraft using degree-distance route definition procedures must file flight plans at least 2 hours before the estimated time of departure.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 4-4-3a5(b).

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A military flight plan proposes route segments that do not coincide with designated ATS routes or a direct course between NAVAIDs. The operation is not one of the special military operations authorized in subparagraph b, but another controller wants to accept it anyway because the route looks workable.
items.para_context: Under 4-4-3, do not accept a military flight plan whose route or route segments do not coincide with designated ATS routes or with a direct course between NAVAIDs unless it is authorized in subparagraph b and meets the listed degree-distance route definition and procedural requirements.

choices.text: Do not accept the flight plan under those circumstances.
Accept it because any military route may use degree-distance fixes if workload permits.
Accept it if the pilot verbally confirms the route after departure.
Accept it only if the aircraft is below FL 180, regardless of authorization.

items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a, nonstandard military routes must be specifically authorized in subparagraph b and meet the listed requirements.

items.instruction: Is this alternative-route directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: When using a charted alternative route because of NAVAID status, use the word "substitute" immediately preceding the alternative route in issuing the clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 4-4-4a.

items.instruction: Which option is one of the listed alternative routes in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 4-4-4, which option is one listed alternative route for aircraft that are not RNAV capable when part of an airway or route is unusable because of NAVAID status?

choices.text: Vectors.
A route based only on pilot preference with no ATC definition.
A route using unpublished waypoints only.
A route defined solely by airline company procedures.

items.explanation: Per 4-4-4d, vectors are one of the listed alternative routes.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A NAVAID outage makes part of an airway unusable for an aircraft that is not RNAV capable. You plan to clear the aircraft via a charted alternative route, but another controller says there is no need to label it specially in the clearance.
items.para_context: Under 4-4-4, when any part of an airway or route is unusable because of NAVAID status, non-RNAV aircraft may be cleared via certain listed alternative routes, and if a charted route is used the word "substitute" must immediately precede that route in the clearance.

choices.text: Issue the clearance using the word "substitute" immediately before the alternative route.
Issue the route without the word "substitute" because the outage itself makes the meaning obvious.
Refuse to issue any alternative route because only RNAV aircraft may be rerouted.
Use only unpublished pilot-proposed routing during NAVAID outages.

items.explanation: Per 4-4-4a, "substitute" must precede the charted alternative route in the clearance.

items.instruction: Is this position-information directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Inform the aircraft of its position at least once before starting final approach.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the body requirement in 5-10-7.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-9-2, Final Approach Course Interception.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That JO reference appears in 5-10-7. The paragraph also cites Para 5-12-10, Elevation Failure.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A radar-approach aircraft is being vectored toward final approach. A trainee wants to wait until the aircraft is already established on final before giving any position information.
items.para_context: Under 5-10-7, the aircraft must be informed of its position at least once before starting final approach.

choices.text: Provide the aircraft with position information before it starts final approach.
Wait until the aircraft is established on final because the paragraph does not require any earlier position report.
Give position information only after the pilot requests it.
Skip position information if glidepath information will be given later.

items.explanation: Per 5-10-7, at least one position report is required before the aircraft starts final approach.

items.instruction: Which item is one listed minimum-use requirement for terminal automation systems?
items.question_text: Under 5-14-4, which item is one of the listed minimum terminal automation system requirements?

choices.text: Aircraft identification should be entered, and automated handoff functions should be used.
Assigned altitude should be updated only when requested by another controller.
Interfaced facilities need not be informed of unscheduled shutdowns.
Flight data may be left active indefinitely when a position stops using the system.

items.explanation: Per 5-14-4c, at a minimum aircraft identification should be entered and automated handoff functions should be used.

items.instruction: Does this automation requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Assigned altitude, if displayed, must be kept current at all times, and climb and descent arrows, where available, must be used to indicate other than level flight.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-4d.

items.instruction: Does this coordination statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Prearranged coordination procedures must be contained in a facility directive in accordance with paragraph 5-4-9, Prearranged Coordination, and FAA Order JO 7210.3, paragraph 3-6-7, Prearranged Coordination.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That coordination requirement appears in 5-14-4f2.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A control position is about to terminate use of the terminal automation system. Another controller wants to shut it down first and coordinate with the affected positions afterward if any issues come up.
items.para_context: Under 5-14-4a, all appropriate positions must be informed before terminating or reinstating use of the terminal automation system at a control position, and when use is terminated all pertinent flight data of that position must be transferred or terminated.

choices.text: Inform all appropriate positions before the change and transfer or terminate the pertinent flight data.
Terminate system use first and coordinate later only if another position complains.
Leave all flight data active because it will clear automatically.
Skip notification if the shutdown is unscheduled.

items.explanation: Per 5-14-4a-b, positions and interfaced facilities are informed before/when automation use changes and pertinent flight data is handled.

items.instruction: Which criterion is one of the listed altitude-assignment standards in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 7-8-5, when altitudes must be assigned to VFR aircraft, which criterion may be used?

choices.text: The MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria.
Only hemispheric VFR cruising altitudes with no exception.
Only the altitude requested by the pilot, regardless of terrain or radar minima.
Only the lowest altitude displayed on a terminal automation system.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-5a, assigned altitudes to VFR aircraft must meet the MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria.

items.instruction: Does this Class C altitude requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Aircraft assigned altitudes that are contrary to 14 CFR section 91.159 must be advised to resume altitudes appropriate for the direction of flight when the altitude is no longer needed for separation, when leaving the outer area, or when terminating Class C service.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 7-8-5b.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A VFR aircraft in Class C was assigned an altitude contrary to the normal direction-of-flight rule for separation. The separation need has ended, but the pilot has not yet been told to return to an appropriate VFR altitude.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-5b, aircraft assigned altitudes contrary to 14 CFR section 91.159 must be advised to resume altitudes appropriate for the direction of flight when the altitude is no longer needed for separation, when leaving the outer area, or when terminating Class C service.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.
Keep the aircraft at the assigned altitude until it leaves your frequency regardless of need.
Wait until the pilot requests a lower or higher altitude.
Terminate Class C service without mentioning the altitude assignment.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-5b, once the separation need ends or the aircraft leaves the relevant area/service, the pilot is advised to resume appropriate VFR altitudes.

items.instruction: Which aircraft pair is one of the listed exceptions in this paragraph?
items.question_text: Under 7-8-6, which aircraft pairing is one of the listed Class C separation exceptions?

choices.text: Hot air balloons and IFR aircraft.
All VFR fixed-wing aircraft and all IFR aircraft.
Any two helicopters regardless of flight rules or service.
Any VFR aircraft and any military aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-6b, hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft.

items.instruction: Does this exception requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate under the exceptions in 7-8-6.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Both subparagraphs in 7-8-6 require traffic information and safety alerts as appropriate.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A VFR helicopter and an IFR helicopter are operating in Class C. Another controller says no separation is required, so no advisory work is needed either.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-6a, VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft, but traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.

choices.text: Issue traffic information and safety alerts as appropriate even though standard separation is not required.
Issue no advisories because the exception removes all controller obligations.
Apply the exception only if both aircraft are fixed-wing.
Terminate Class C service to avoid using the exception.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-6, the exception removes the separation requirement for certain pairings but not the requirement to issue traffic information and safety alerts as appropriate.

items.instruction: Does this adjacent-airport Class C statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Aircraft that will penetrate Class C airspace after departing controlled airports within or adjacent to Class C airspace must be provided the same services as those aircraft departing the primary airport.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That requirement appears in 7-8-7a.

items.instruction: Does this procedural-document statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Procedures for handling controlled-airport departures that will penetrate Class C airspace must be covered in a letter of agreement or a facility directive, as appropriate.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 7-8-7a, procedures for that situation must be covered in an LOA or a facility directive, as appropriate.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A controlled airport adjacent to Class C launches an aircraft that will penetrate the Class C airspace after departure. A trainee suggests treating it differently from primary-airport departures because it originated outside the primary airport.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-7a, aircraft that will penetrate Class C airspace after departing controlled airports within or adjacent to Class C airspace must be provided the same services as those aircraft departing the primary airport.

choices.text: Provide the same services that would be given to an aircraft departing the primary airport.
Withhold the normal Class C services because the aircraft did not depart the primary airport.
Wait until the aircraft requests Class C service after penetration begins.
Handle it only under uncontrolled-airport Letter to Airmen procedures.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-7a, those controlled-airport departures receive the same services as departures from the primary airport.

items.question_text: According to the note in 2-8-1, what is the minimum readout value of older RVR equipment?
items.explanation: The note states that older equipment minimum readout value is 600 feet.

choices.text: 600 feet.
100 feet.
800 feet.
1,000 feet.

items.question_text: Under 2-8-1, if RVR equipment is operational, RVR values are furnished for the runway in use irrespective of subsequent operation or nonoperation of navigational or visual aids used in applying RVR as takeoff or landing minima.
items.explanation: That statement appears in the body of 2-8-1.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-1-14, which action should be taken to the extent possible when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-14a, avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.

choices.text: Avoid requiring aircraft to come to a full stop while taxiing.
Require every taxiing aircraft to stop for visibility checks.
Suspend all taxi operations immediately.
Delay all departures until the surface is washed.

items.question_text: Under 3-1-14, provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures to the extent possible when volcanic ash is present on the airport surface.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 3-1-14b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 3-6-1, how must ASDE systems with safety logic be operated?
items.explanation: Per 3-6-1b1, ASDE systems with safety logic must be operated continuously.

choices.text: Continuously.
Only between sunset and sunrise.
Only when visibility is below the most distant point in the active movement area.
Only during periods of arrival rushes.

items.question_text: Under 3-6-1, the operational status of ASDE systems must be determined during the relief briefing or as soon as possible after assuming responsibility for the associated position.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 3-6-1a.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-4-3, what is the maximum distance between fixes used to define the route below FL 180?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a3(a), the limit below FL 180 is 80 miles.

choices.text: 80 miles.
75 miles.
180 miles.
260 miles.

items.question_text: Under 4-4-3, degree-distance fixes used to define a route are considered compulsory reporting points, except that ATC may authorize an aircraft to omit reports when traffic conditions permit.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 4-4-3a4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 4-4-4, which option is one of the listed alternative routes for aircraft that are not RNAV capable during a NAVAID outage?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-4, listed alternatives include charted substitute routes, routes defined by radials/courses/azimuths, routes direct to or between NAVAIDs, and vectors.

choices.text: A route defined by specifying NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.
Any unpublished route proposed informally by the pilot.
A route using random off-airway waypoints only.
A route defined only after departure by company dispatch.

items.question_text: Under the note in 4-4-4, area navigation aircraft that will proceed to the NAVAID location must be informed of the NAVAID outage.
items.explanation: That note appears in 4-4-4.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-10-7, when must the aircraft be informed of its position?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires informing the aircraft of its position at least once before starting final approach.

choices.text: At least once before starting final approach.
Only after final approach has started.
Only at decision height.
Only after touchdown.

items.question_text: One acceptable phraseology form in 5-10-7 is "(number) MILES (direction) OF (airport name) AIRPORT ON DOWNWIND/BASE LEG."
items.explanation: That phraseology appears in 5-10-7.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 5-14-4, what must be done before terminating or reinstating use of the terminal automation system at a control position?
items.explanation: Per 5-14-4a, all appropriate positions must be informed before the change.

choices.text: Inform all appropriate positions.
Wait until the change is complete, then advise only if asked.
Terminate all flight data first and coordinate later.
Notify only the supervisor, not other affected positions.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-4, assigned altitude, if displayed, must be kept current at all times, and climb and descent arrows, where available, must be used to indicate other than level flight.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-4d.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-8-5, what must assigned altitudes to VFR aircraft meet when altitude assignments are necessary?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-5a, assigned altitudes must meet the MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria.

choices.text: The MVA, MSA, or minimum IFR altitude criteria.
Only 14 CFR section 91.159 cruising altitude rules with no exception.
Only the highest altitude on the display.
Only the altitude requested by the pilot.

items.question_text: Under 7-8-5, aircraft assigned altitudes contrary to 14 CFR section 91.159 must be advised to resume altitudes appropriate for the direction of flight when the altitude is no longer needed for separation, when leaving the outer area, or when terminating Class C service.
items.explanation: That statement appears in 7-8-5b.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-8-6, which aircraft pairing is specifically listed as not requiring separation?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-6b, hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Hot air balloons and IFR aircraft.
All VFR aircraft and all IFR aircraft.
Any two aircraft when traffic information is issued.
VFR fixed-wing aircraft and IFR fixed-wing aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 7-8-6, traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in both subparagraphs of 7-8-6.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 7-8-7, how must aircraft that will penetrate Class C airspace after departing controlled airports within or adjacent to Class C airspace be handled?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-7a, they must be provided the same services as aircraft departing the primary airport.

choices.text: They must be provided the same services as aircraft departing the primary airport.
They are handled only after they request Class C service from the air.
They are handled under uncontrolled-airport procedures only.
They receive advisories only if they are military aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 7-8-7, aircraft departing uncontrolled airports within Class C airspace must be handled using procedures advertised in a Letter to Airmen.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 7-8-7b.

choices.text: True
False
